triage_context.environment: Internet-facing web service (HTTP untrusted). TMI collaborative threat-modeling platform: unauth OAuth2/OIDC/SAML endpoints, authenticated REST/WS, untrusted document bytes in an extraction pipeline, worker-published NATS messages as an interior untrusted boundary (compromised worker = designed-for attacker per THREAT_MODEL.md T19).

findings.title: SAML matches users by email only — missing the cross-provider conflict check the OAuth path has
preconditions: SAML enabled with at least one IdP configured
attacker can obtain a signed assertion bearing the victim's email from ANY configured IdP (multi-tenant IdP, user-editable mail attribute, or less-trusted secondary IdP)
victim account exists (e.g. OAuth-created)
POST /saml/acs reachable (pre-auth by design); SAML hardcodes EmailVerified=true
findings.rationale:
saml_manager.go:260 matches users by bare email via GetUserByEmail with no provider binding and mints tokens for the match (saml_manager.go:249), reachable pre-auth via POST /saml/acs. The OAuth path explicitly rejects this exact case as account takeover (handlers_oauth_user.go:144-152, errCrossProviderConflict, issue #290 comment describes the attack verbatim); the SAML path skips that tiered matcher entirely and hardcodes EmailVerified=true (saml_manager.go:280).

Full takeover of any OAuth-created account by anyone who can make any configured SAML IdP emit the target's email. IdP-side precondition tempers to MEDIUM; operator-stated ATO threat raises to HIGH.
findings.owner_hint: component: auth/; no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: No per-component NATS subject scoping; any worker can publish results for any job
preconditions: code execution on an extraction worker (designed-for attacker via hostile-document parsing) or any pod with reach to the cluster-internal NATS service
NATS at shipped default config — no authorization block (deployments/k8s/platform/nats.yml); workers connect credential-less (internal/worker/nats.go:45)
victim JobIDs — effectively free, observed on unscoped subjects
findings.threat_match: Cross-user threat-model data leakage (T19 interior worker boundary)
findings.rationale:
Result consumer binds jobs.result.> (result_consumer.go:175) and handleResult/MarkTerminal trust the payload JobID with no publisher-identity, signature, or subject-vs-payload check; MarkTerminal even bare-inserts terminal rows for unknown ids. NATS ships with zero authorization and workers connect credential-less, so a compromised worker can forge terminal state for arbitrary jobs, fire other users' webhooks, suppress real results (the forged result consumes the emit-once transition), and delete arbitrary payload blobs via Output.ResultRef.

Mechanically MEDIUM (one precondition: worker compromise), raised to HIGH because the compromised worker is the exact designed-for attacker at the boundary this control is missing from.
findings.owner_hint: component: api/ (+ deployments/k8s/platform NATS config); no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: Result envelope fields trusted without validation (unbounded ReasonDetail persisted and served to clients)
preconditions: NATS publish access (worker compromise or unscoped NATS reach)
worker compromise reachable via hostile document submitted by any authenticated user
victim JobIDs free once inside
valid object_ref for blob deletion (ResultRef unbound to job)
findings.rationale:
No ValidateResult exists (pkg/jobenvelope/validate.go covers Job only — the design spec's promised envelope validation is unimplemented). ReasonDetail persists unbounded into the NullableDBText access_reason_detail column (document_store_gorm.go:630, models.go:400, GORM hooks bypassed) and is served RAW to authenticated clients when reason_code="other" (access_diagnostics.go:118-120). Forged envelopes flip any document's access_status, fire spoofed webhooks, and trigger DeletePayload on attacker-chosen refs.

Base MEDIUM, one-step T19 raise to HIGH; fix pairs with f001 (subject scoping) plus field validation/caps in the consumer.
findings.owner_hint: component: api/; no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: OAuth BaseProvider raw http.Client without redirect blocking; auth/ escaped the SafeHTTPClient lint guard
preconditions: a configured non-OIDC OAuth provider whose endpoint is hostile/compromised (declared in scope), OR an admin-settings write pointing token_url/userinfo at internal targets (MutabilityHot DB settings, non-emptiness validation only)
trigger: any unauthenticated /oauth2/authorize -> callback flow
server network position reaches internal targets
307/308 redirect re-sends the client_secret POST body
findings.rationale:
auth/provider.go:130 builds a redirect-following raw client used for token exchange (POSTing client_secret, line 219) and userinfo (319/339); worse, the standard exchange at line 167 uses bare http.DefaultClient (no timeout) since oauth2.HTTPClient is never injected. Provider endpoint URLs are runtime-mutable DB settings (classification_registry.go:286-290) validated only for non-emptiness. The repo's mandatory SafeHTTPClient control (redirect-block + IP pinning) is enforced by a lint that scans only api/*.go (scripts/check-direct-http-client.py:66), so the auth package silently escaped the #345 migration while comparable code (api/content_oauth_provider.go:227) was migrated.

SSRF into the internal network plus client_secret/auth-code exfiltration enabling forged exchanges; ATO threat-match raises to HIGH.
findings.owner_hint: component: auth/ (+ scripts/check-direct-http-client.py); no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: Unbounded HTML parse-tree recursion runs in the MONOLITH content pipeline — single hostile page can fatally crash the API server
preconditions: authenticated account (registering HTTP content sources is an ordinary capability)
attacker-staged text/html endpoint
page within the 50 MiB fetch cap (content_source_http.go:14) carrying ~10-17M nesting levels
default monolith pipeline wiring (cmd/server/main.go:1432) — no flag required
findings.rationale:
Verification corrected the scan: the cited api/timmy_content_provider_http.go copy is test-only dead code, but the identical pkg/extract copy is LIVE in the monolith via the content pipeline (main.go:1432 -> content_pipeline.go:248, reached from access_poller.go:206 and timmy_content_provider.go:106). HTMLExtractor implements no BoundedExtractor so the deadline wrapper is skipped (and could not stop stack growth anyway); Go stack exhaustion is an unrecoverable fatal error. Aggravator: the access poller re-fetches registered sources, so the server can crash-loop on restart until the source row is purged.

MEDIUM (authenticated-only, availability impact). Whether a 50 MiB payload clears the 1 GB goroutine-stack ceiling straddles the margin — recommend a human-built PoC in a sandbox.
findings.owner_hint: component: pkg/extract/ (fix also dedupes api/timmy_content_provider_http.go); no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: SAML LogoutRequest signature not verified — unauthenticated targeted session invalidation
preconditions: SAML enabled and a provider configured
IdP entityID (public metadata)
victim email/NameID (OSINT; endpoint returns success even for unknown users, enabling enumeration)
victim has active sessions
findings.rationale:
ProcessLogoutRequest (auth/saml/provider.go:259-286) performs no signature verification — only an issuer string match against the public IdP entityID and a NameID presence check — while the login path uses ParseXMLResponse with full validation. The handler comment at handlers_saml.go:320 falsely claims signature verification; GET/POST /saml/slo is unauthenticated by design (security:[] in the OpenAPI spec) and sessions are invalidated for the attacker-supplied NameID (handlers_saml.go:330-347). No IssueInstant or replay protection.

Unauthenticated and trivially forgeable, but impact is repeatable forced logout (availability harassment), not session theft — derived MEDIUM; the claimed HIGH overweights mechanism vs impact.
findings.owner_hint: component: auth/saml/; no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: Mid-session role revocation does not stop WS broadcast delivery — revoked user keeps reading confidential diagram edits up to JWT lifetime
preconditions: prior legitimate access to the threat model
active WS session at the moment of revocation
revocation occurs mid-session
session kept alive within the 300s heartbeat; exposure capped at remaining JWT lifetime (default 3600s, config.go:437)
collaborators actively editing during the window
findings.rationale:
Broadcast delivery sends diagram_operation_event content to every client in s.Clients with no per-recipient re-check (websocket.go:1549-1556, 2729-2802; explicit trust comment at 2567-2568). The WritePump heartbeat (3544) re-validates only JWT expiry — not blacklist or role state — and no code path anywhere prunes WS session membership on ACL change (the only CloseSession caller is the host leave-session endpoint). Mutations ARE re-checked per operation, confirming the read-side gap is an oversight. Exposure window is the remaining JWT lifetime, not the 300s the scan assumed, since active sessions stay alive.

Base LOW raised one step to MEDIUM: the leaked content is exactly the operator's top-stated threat at exactly the moment trust is withdrawn.
findings.owner_hint: component: api/ (websocket); no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: Secret-classified settings stored plaintext when encryption unconfigured; fail-open with log-only enforcement
preconditions: deployment without the settings encryption key despite the production ERROR log (fail-open; encryptor init failure also fails open per cmd/server/main.go:628-633)
secret-classified settings actually written to system_settings via the admin API (config_handlers.go:638) or dbtool import (cmd/dbtool/config.go:91)
separate DB/backup compromise (independent event, but explicitly in the operator threat model)
auth.jwt.secret specifically DB-resident for the maximal token-forgery chain
findings.rationale:
SettingsService.Set encrypts only when the encryptor is configured and enabled; the Secret classification drives API masking, not write-time enforcement. The startup check is documented as never aborting (startup_checks.go:26-27) and ReEncryptAll is a manual admin endpoint that errors unless encryption was pre-enabled — plaintext secrets written before enablement persist silently (Decrypt passthrough). At stake: auth.jwt.secret (token forgery), OAuth client secrets, SAML SP private keys, vault token, LLM keys. One verifier dissented (intentional opt-in design); majority held that a deliberately non-enforcing control around a condition the code itself labels SECURITY WARNING, with DB/backup compromise in scope, is a real gap.

Two stacked independent preconditions keep it MEDIUM.
findings.owner_hint: component: api/ + internal/crypto; no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: Unbounded io.ReadAll / uncapped JSON decode on OAuth provider responses (gzip-amplified, unauth-drivable)
preconditions: a configured OAuth provider is hostile, compromised, or MITM'd (operator-configured endpoints; cannot be attacker-pointed directly)
attacker self-initiates callback flows (controls own PKCE verifier) — no TMI authentication needed
gzip-bombed bodies and/or concurrent flows to beat the 10s bandwidth-x-time bound
findings.rationale:
io.ReadAll with no LimitReader at auth/provider.go:227 and :327, uncapped json.Decode at :233/:333, body duplicated into log+error strings (~3x peak allocation), no transport cap; the only bound is the 10s client timeout, which limits time not bytes, and fetchEndpoint sets no Accept-Encoding so Go transparently gunzips — a small compressed response expands to multi-GB allocation in-window. The repo's own readCappedBody/newCappedReadCloser helpers (api/safe_http_client.go:338-363) are unused on this path. Reachable from the public callback handler on every login (handlers_token.go:141/177).

Availability-only impact behind a subverted-provider precondition lands MEDIUM.
findings.owner_hint: component: auth/; no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: Provider-secret masking list diverges from the classification registry — content_oauth client_secret returned plaintext from the DB path
preconditions: step-up-gated admin session (or access to admin response logs / HAR captures / proxy traffic)
a content_oauth.providers.<id>.client_secret row manually PUT into the DB (config-defined providers take the masked config branch)
the DB row holds a live secret — runtime loads content OAuth providers from config only (content_source_holder.go:252), so a DB copy may be inert
findings.rationale:
isProviderSecretKey (config_handlers.go:27-72) requires a prefix from {auth.oauth.providers., auth.saml.providers.} plus one of four suffixes, and is the ONLY mask on the DB-sourced path (lines 422, 549). content_oauth.providers.<id>.client_secret is Secret:true (migratable_settings.go:664-668) and VisibilityAdminOnly (classification_registry.go:296) but its prefix is absent — a DB-stored value returns plaintext in GET and the merged LIST, violating the registry's own contract that the Secret flag drives API-response masking. Verification was 2-1: the dissent (DB copy is unconsumed dead data) is reflected in needs_manual_test.

Admin-tier readback only; LOW. Fix: drive masking from ClassificationFor(key).Secret instead of a parallel hardcoded list.
findings.owner_hint: component: api/ + internal/config; no CODEOWNERS; top committer: Eric Fitzgerald (sole committer)

findings.title: Unescaped image alt text in DOCX markdown
findings.rationale: Same unescaped-markdown extraction helper as f021; one escaping fix covers both.

findings.title: userAuthTime exists-flag ignored in step-up middleware
findings.rationale: Deterministic cluster with f014 (same file/category, lines within 10).

findings.title: Dual-auth HMAC-or-JWT on delivery-status endpoint
findings.rationale: Same dual-auth design surface as f028.

findings.title: ResultRef not bound to JobID before Object Store deletion
findings.rationale: 2-1 FP: the unbound delete exists, but the designed-for attacker (compromised worker) already holds direct unrestricted delete/overwrite on the same TMI_PAYLOADS bucket via its own credential-less NATS connection (internal/worker/nats.go:57,78,102) — the deputy grants no new capability in any shipped configuration. Becomes meaningful only after per-component NATS scoping (f001's fix) lands; bind ResultRef to JobID as part of that work.

findings.title: VisibilityInternal LIST/PUT asymmetry
findings.rationale: All VisibilityInternal keys are CategoryBootstrap and never legitimately reach the DB; PUT of real bootstrap secrets is 409-blocked as config-controlled; the only exposable value is one the same step-up admin wrote. Documented known inconsistency (classification_registry.go:177-185).

findings.title: XML depth limit bypassed inside DecodeElement subtrees
findings.rationale: All five DecodeElement call sites decode into plain strings, for which encoding/xml skips nested children via the ITERATIVE Decoder.Skip (CVE-2022-28131 fix) — no recursion occurs at all; maxUnmarshalDepth=10000 errors cleanly as backstop; boundedReader caps volume.

findings.title: Delegated-token GCM encryption lacks AAD row binding
findings.rationale: 2-1 FP: nil AAD confirmed and ciphertext grafting would work (picker_token_handler.go:164 even returns plaintext tokens), but exploitation requires a key-less DB-write primitive that doesn't exist anywhere in the codebase (all GORM-parameterized; admin API list/delete only). Still recommended as cheap hardening: AAD = userID:providerID.

findings.title: Step-up route table fail-open
findings.rationale: The router registrations and the spec consumed by BuildStepUpRouteTable are the SAME oapi-codegen artifact (api/api.go) — spec/router drift is structurally impossible; no admin write is registered outside codegen; the default policy auto-covers future spec additions.

findings.title: WS ticket session_id cross-check optional
findings.rationale: The ticket conveys identity only; the session joined derives from the URL path and post-upgrade ValidateSessionAccess (websocket.go:1670) independently re-authorizes the user against that resource. Cross-session replay grants nothing the user couldn't get by minting a ticket directly. 32-byte random, single-use, 30s TTL.

findings.title: fixOwnerField fabricates User objects from string /owner values
findings.rationale: Intentional string-to-User coercion for an owner-authorized transfer: /owner is OwnerOnly and triple-gated; the store resolves the identifier against EXISTING users only and rolls back on ErrUserNotFound; audit/broadcast/webhook all fire only after a successful store write. No consumer acts on the fabricated value; actor gains no privilege.

findings.title: OIDC discovery raw http.Client
findings.rationale: Discover() is called only from startup validation with file/env config; the DB-mutable issuer setting never reaches the discovery client (no runtime code constructs one); the response is never reflected and discovery-doc endpoints are never adopted. Trusted-operator startup input. Fix as cheap consistency with #345 if desired.

findings.title: Owner-change detection uses struct equality
findings.rationale: Struct equality over all five User fields is a strict superset of canonical identity — a real owner change (differing provider_id) can never compare equal, so the gate-skip is logically impossible. Only failure mode is fail-closed over-triggering; enrichment never touches Owner; transfer rule keys canonically.

findings.title: Status-update HMAC signs body only
findings.rationale: Secret resolved via the target delivery's own SubscriptionID confines replay to one subscription, whose receiver holds the secret and can mint signatures anyway; terminal states immutable; the only beneficiary needs a TLS break for a captured sample and gains a status-telemetry nudge. Hardening: bind delivery_id/timestamp into the MAC (the GET path already binds the ID).

findings.title: Participants broadcast hardcodes 'writer'
findings.rationale: Write-only display metadata; clients cannot inject participants_update (protocol violation, websocket_handlers.go:83-88); all mutations independently authorized per operation; REST path resolves true permissions. Cosmetic role-badge inaccuracy only.

findings.title: Unescaped hyperlink targets in extracted DOCX markdown
findings.rationale: Only production sink is the fenced Timmy LLM context (timmy_context_builder.go:99); no endpoint returns extracted markdown or chunk text; no markdown-to-HTML renderer exists in the repo; the dev Note-dumper path is blocked in production builds (config.go:982). Residual is prompt-injection territory (excluded; modeled as T6). Cheap immunization: escape ]()[ and validate URL schemes at extraction.

findings.title: Reserved SourceURL/SourceSecretRef envelope fields unenforced
findings.rationale: Zero consumers repo-wide; envelopes are produced server-side only; documented intentional forward-compatibility. Keep as the design-review tripwire already recorded in THREAT_MODEL.md open questions.

findings.title: XLSX caps decoupled
findings.rationale: 2-1 FP: majority found all allocation paths bounded by configured constants (cell cap trips before append; excelize part limits gate shared strings; markdown cap on output). DISSENT worth an engineering look: one verifier argued the XLSX path bypasses MaxCompressionRatio and per-cell shared-string re-allocation could amplify (depends on excelize internals unverifiable from this repo).

findings.title: WS Origin prefix matching
findings.rationale: Stronger refutation than the scan's: WS paths authenticate EXCLUSIVELY via a ?ticket= query param (jwt_auth.go:29-41) — cookie/Bearer fallbacks are unreachable for WS — and tickets are unobtainable cross-site (authenticated REST mint + SameSite=Lax cookie + exact-match CORS). A cross-origin upgrade dies 401 before CheckOrigin matters. Still fix the HasPrefix to exact match: it is one cookie-policy change away from load-bearing.

findings.title: Token to Confluence apiBase
findings.rationale: apiBase is a compile-time constant (https://api.atlassian.com), unexported, no setter; only test files override it. The misconfiguration premise does not exist.

findings.title: Result-consumer lookup/terminal-flip ordering
findings.rationale: Emit-once is DB-enforced (guarded UPDATE re-evaluated under row lock + OnConflict DoNothing insert); the sentinel arises only on the bare-insert path and is dropped by later lookups; the ordering is deliberate (#438) so transient failures redeliver without consuming the emit signal. Worst case: one lost webhook on process crash — not duplication, not corruption.

findings.title: Delivery-status GET HMAC signs delivery ID only
findings.rationale: No second consumer of the ID-only scheme exists (the other HMAC verifications sign JSON bodies — format-disjoint, a bare UUID can never collide); the secret is scoped to the target delivery's own subscription, whose receiver is exactly the authorized party; empty signatures route to the fail-closed JWT leg. Intended dual-auth design.

findings.title: PKCE get-then-delete race
findings.rationale: Store is a mutex-guarded in-memory map (not shared Redis as the scan assumed); a won race only duplicates the PUBLIC S256 challenge; exchange still requires the secret code_verifier plus a single-use code; reaching the window requires the victim's already-consumed state nonce. Theoretical-only TOCTOU.

findings.title: PKCE challenge exposure in content-OAuth flow
findings.rationale: Error paths return static codes or bodyless 500s with internals logged server-side only; the verifier exists only in the Redis state payload and the server-to-server token exchange; the challenge is public per RFC 7636 design.

findings.title: No per-operation cell cap in 64KB WS frames
findings.rationale: 2-1 FP: volumetric load by an authorized writer achievable via REST anyway. ENGINEERING NOTE from the dissent: each cell op triggers a full-diagram DB read + two audit serializations + write under the HUB-GLOBAL updateMutex (websocket.go:52,341) — O(N^2) write amplification per frame that serializes diagram updates server-wide. Worth fixing as a performance bug even though not a security boundary.

findings.title: Raw-HTML fallback on html.Parse error
findings.rationale: html.Parse implements the WHATWG error-tolerant algorithm and never errors on content — only on reader I/O failures, impossible with strings.NewReader. The fallback is dead defensive code, and consumers are non-HTML-sensitive (embedding/RAG text).
